message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #1 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: METHODOLOGY §7 and GOVERNANCE §2 body text claim pre-registration is "RFC-3161 timestamped"; the engine ships only an HMAC authority (RFC-3161 is pluggable, not shipped/default)
DIMENSION: docs vs code drift / over-claims
SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: docs/METHODOLOGY.md:130-132; docs/GOVERNANCE.md:55-62 METHODOLOGY.md:131
DESCRIPTION: Normative body text presents RFC-3161 timestamping as the mechanism actually in force. METHODOLOGY §7 L130-132: "Pre-registration: hypotheses (data-surfaced or claim-derived) are locked and RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation." GOVERNANCE §2 L55-62 step 3: hypotheses are "RFC-3161 timestamped against a trusted timestamp authority." But the engine ships exactly one timestamp authority — LocalHmacAuthority (on-box HMAC) — and no RFC-3161 TSA client (grep of src/ finds RFC-3161 only in comments/docstrings as a pluggable option; preregistration.py:130 LocalHmacAuthority is the only concrete authority). The runnable example (examples/minimal_study.py:38) and all defaults use LocalHmacAuthority. GOVERNANCE §2's own implementation-status note (L64-81) and ADR-0009 correctly hedge this ("ships one real, data-sovereign authority LocalHmacAuthority... and the Protocol so a study can plug an RFC-3161 TSA"), but the §7/§2 body claims read as if RFC-3161 (third-party non-repudiation) is what happens. HMAC is local tamper-evidence relative to an on-box secret — explicitly NOT the third-party time attestation RFC-3161 provides. The mismatch matters because RFC-3161 is exactly the non-repudiation property a hostile-reviewer audience would key on.
SUGGESTED FIX: In the METHODOLOGY §7 bullet and GOVERNANCE §2 step 3, change the flat "RFC-3161 timestamped" to the shipped default with the pluggable option, e.g. "locked and cryptographically timestamped before confirmation (on-box HMAC by default — local tamper-evidence; an RFC-3161 TSA is pluggable for third-party non-repudiation)." Keep the body text consistent with the existing honest-scope notes.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this claim by reading the actual code and documentation.

content.content:
120	> (measurement → interpretation) and records the source measurement's ref. A study applies these
121	> types where judgment enters: LLM output from the reasoning seam is *raw judgment* and must be
122	> wrapped as an `Interpretation` (`judged_by` = the model id), never passed where a `Measurement`
123	> is expected. The engine enforces the direction structurally at the type boundary; it does not,
124	> and cannot, retro-classify arbitrary study values it never sees.
125	
126	## 7. Reproducibility
127	
128	- **Determinism:** fixed, documented seeds; inputs hashed; model names + versions recorded.
129	- **Provenance:** an append-only audit trail records every action before the next executes.
130	- **Pre-registration:** hypotheses (data-surfaced or claim-derived) are locked and
131	  RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation. This is honestly described as an adaptive
132	  design with a timestamped lock — not as predictions guessed before any data.
133	- **Reproducibility package:** data, code, configuration, and logs are published with
134	  findings so any reviewer can reconstruct the full decision history.
135	
136	## 8. Scoped-independence caveat
137	
138	When the baseline is built from text or artifacts that an external source also authored,
139	the baseline is independent of that source's **judgments**, but not of its **wording or
140	framing**. This is an acceptable, necessary compromise — but it is claimed as *scoped*
141	independence, stated explicitly, and is one of the reasons `indeterminate` exists.
142

45	  `run_id` as `thread_id`).
46	
47	**State-shape requirement for cloners:** gate decisions are returned as
48	`{"gate_decisions": [record]}` partial updates, so a study's graph state must declare
49	`gate_decisions` with a list/append reducer — otherwise each gate would overwrite the prior
50	one and the append-only trail would lose all but the last decision.
51	
52	## 2. Pre-registration
53	
54	Before any confirmatory test runs, the relevant hypotheses are:
55	
56	1. made specific and typed (what is claimed, the test, the data, the decision rule),
57	2. content-hashed and committed,
58	3. **RFC-3161 timestamped** against a trusted timestamp authority.
59	
60	This is honestly characterized as a **timestamped adaptive design**: methods are fixed
61	first; the data informs the questions; the questions are then locked and timestamped before
62	they are tested. It is not claimed to be "predictions registered before any data."
63	
64	> **Implementation status (engine):** pre-registration is enforced by construction **in the
65	> confirmatory runners** (`adjudicate`, `discover_and_confirm`; ADR-0009). They verify, via a
66	> supplied `TimestampAuthority`, that a hypothesis's proof **binds its content and id** (the
67	> canonical digest of its decision-bearing fields), that the attested **timestamp is
68	> verifiable**, and that the lock **precedes confirmation** — a hand-set sentinel string, a
69	> post-lock content/id swap, a forged proof, or a lock dated at/after the ordering instant are
70	> all refused. (In adjudication the ordering instant is captured *before the baseline build*, so
71	> a claim-derived hypothesis must be locked before the baseline exists; in discovery a
72	> data-surfaced hypothesis is necessarily locked inside the runner, so there the *content*
73	> binding is the load-bearing guarantee — see ADR-0009.) The engine ships one real,
74	> data-sovereign authority (`LocalHmacAuthority`, on-box HMAC) and the Protocol so a study can
75	> plug an RFC-3161 TSA. Honest scope: the local authority is tamper-evidence *relative to an
76	> on-box secret*, not third-party non-repudiation. The lower-level confirm *primitives*
77	> (`confirm_whole_corpus`/`confirm_unit_level`) gate on the cheap presence predicate by default
78	> and take an optional `authority=` to opt into the full check; a study that confirms outside a
79	> runner must pass it (or call `require_preregistered` itself). `Hypothesis.locked` is only a
80	> presence predicate; `methodology.preregistration.require_preregistered` is the
81	> methodology-grade check.
82	
83	## 3. Provenance
84	
85	An **append-only** audit trail (W3C PROV-DM style) records every action before the next
86	executes. No step can be retroactively removed or reordered. This is the spine of
87	reproducibility and of the independence claim.
88	
89	> **Implementation status (engine):** wired (ADR-0010). `assay_engine.provenance.ProvenanceTrail`

content.text: The grep confirms: RFC-3161 appears only in docstrings/comments as a pluggable option. `LocalHmacAuthority` is the only concrete authority class. Let me verify the example and confirm the assessor's facts.

content.content:
125	            f"digest ({exc}); pre-registration requires reproducibly hashable content "
126	            "(no non-finite floats or unhashable leaves in params)"
127	        ) from None
128	
129	
130	class LocalHmacAuthority:
131	    """A real, on-box timestamp authority binding a digest and an instant under a secret key.
132	
133	    ``stamp`` issues ``local-hmac:v1:<instant-iso>|<hmac-hex>`` where the MAC is
134	    ``HMAC-SHA256(secret, digest || instant)``. ``verify`` recomputes the MAC over the digest
135	    the engine derived from the *current* hypothesis and the instant carried in the proof, and
136	    constant-time compares. The proof therefore verifies iff the content is unchanged, the
137	    instant is unchanged, and the same secret is used — without the secret it cannot be forged
138	    for arbitrary content or time.
139	
140	    Honest scope: this is local tamper-evidence, not third-party non-repudiation — the holder of
141	    ``secret`` could backdate (see module docstring). Use an RFC-3161 authority when you need a
142	    third party to vouch for the time.
143	    """
144	
145	    authority_id = "local-hmac"
146	
147	    def __init__(self, secret: bytes) -> None:
148	        if not isinstance(secret, (bytes, bytearray)) or len(secret) < 16:
149	            raise ValueError("LocalHmacAuthority secret must be >= 16 bytes of key material")
150	        self._secret = bytes(secret)
151	
152	    def _mac(self, digest: str, instant_iso: str) -> str:
153	        msg = canonical_json(["local-hmac:v1", digest, instant_iso]).encode("utf-8")
154	        return hmac.new(self._secret, msg, "sha256").hexdigest()

content.text: All of the assessor's facts are confirmed. Let me also check the ADR-0009 to confirm it hedges correctly, and double check there is no shipped RFC-3161 client anywhere.

content.text:
Confirmed. No RFC-3161 client is shipped — only `LocalHmacAuthority` is a concrete authority. The body text claims are verified as the assessor described.

This is a genuine docs-vs-code drift / over-claim. The body text in METHODOLOGY §7 (L130-132) and GOVERNANCE §2 step 3 (L58) flatly states hypotheses are "RFC-3161 timestamped," but the engine ships only on-box HMAC. RFC-3161 provides third-party non-repudiation (the exact property a hostile reviewer keys on); HMAC is on-box tamper-evidence where the secret-holder can backdate. The implementation-status notes (GOVERNANCE L64-81) and ADR-0009 are honest, but the normative body text is not — and a reader takes the body text as the claim of record.

This is a documentation accuracy defect, not a code defect. Severity MEDIUM is slightly aggressive for a production-readiness gate since the codebase already contains correct hedges nearby; I'd calibrate to LOW. The defect is real but partially mitigated by adjacent honest-scope notes that contradict the over-claim.

input.reasoning: Confirmed as a genuine docs-vs-code over-claim. The engine ships exactly one concrete timestamp authority — LocalHmacAuthority (on-box HMAC) — and no RFC-3161 TSA client. grep of src/ finds RFC-3161 only in docstrings/comments describing it as a pluggable option; the only concrete authority class in preregistration.py is LocalHmacAuthority (line 130), and the runnable example (examples/minimal_study.py:36) instantiates LocalHmacAuthority as its only authority. Despite this, two pieces of normative body text present RFC-3161 as the mechanism in force: METHODOLOGY.md §7 L130-131 ("hypotheses ... are locked and RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation") and GOVERNANCE.md §2 step 3 L58 ("RFC-3161 timestamped against a trusted timestamp authority"). The distinction is substantive, not stylistic: RFC-3161 is third-party non-repudiation; HMAC is local tamper-evidence relative to an on-box secret whose holder can backdate (the code's own docstring at preregistration.py:140-142 says exactly this). A hostile-reviewer audience keys precisely on the non-repudiation property the body text falsely implies is shipped.\n\nWhy I down-calibrate from MEDIUM to LOW: the codebase is already substantially honest about this. GOVERNANCE §2's implementation-status note (L64-81) explicitly states the engine "ships one real, data-sovereign authority (LocalHmacAuthority, on-box HMAC) and the Protocol so a study can plug an RFC-3161 TSA" and that "the local authority is tamper-evidence relative to an on-box secret, not third-party non-repudiation." ADR-0009 and the preregistration.py module docstring carry the same hedge. So the over-claim is a localized inconsistency between two body sentences and their own adjacent honest-scope notes, not a systemic deception. It is a real production-readiness/credibility defect (misleading normative text in shipped docs) but does not affect engine behavior, correctness, or security, and is contradicted in-document — hence LOW, not MEDIUM. The suggested fix (replace flat "RFC-3161 timestamped" with "cryptographically timestamped — on-box HMAC by default; RFC-3161 TSA pluggable for third-party non-repudiation") is correct and minimal.
input.evidence:
Over-claim in body text — docs/METHODOLOGY.md:130-131: "Pre-registration: hypotheses (data-surfaced or claim-derived) are locked and RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation." docs/GOVERNANCE.md:58 step 3: "RFC-3161 timestamped against a trusted timestamp authority."

Code reality — only LocalHmacAuthority is concrete (src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py:130). grep -rn RFC-3161 over src/ returns only docstring/comment mentions as a pluggable option (preregistration.py:22,30,141; discovery.py:89; adjudication.py:137); no asn1/TimeStampReq/TSA client exists in src/. Class scan of preregistration.py yields TimestampAuthority(Protocol), StampingAuthority(Protocol), and LocalHmacAuthority as the only authority — no RFC-3161 implementation. examples/minimal_study.py:36 uses AUTHORITY = LocalHmacAuthority(...) as the sole authority.

Contradicted by adjacent honest notes — GOVERNANCE.md:73-76 ("ships one real, data-sovereign authority (LocalHmacAuthority, on-box HMAC) and the Protocol so a study can plug an RFC-3161 TSA... the local authority is tamper-evidence relative to an on-box secret, not third-party non-repudiation") and preregistration.py:140-142 docstring (same scope). The defect is the body text disagreeing with the doc's own status note.

content.text:
Verified. The finding is real but I recalibrated severity from MEDIUM to LOW.

Summary: The assessor's facts all check out. `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/docs/METHODOLOGY.md:130-131` and `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/docs/GOVERNANCE.md:58` present "RFC-3161 timestamped" as the active mechanism, but the engine ships only `LocalHmacAuthority` (on-box HMAC) at `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py:130` — RFC-3161 exists in source only as docstring mentions of a pluggable option, with no TSA client. The example at `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/examples/minimal_study.py:36` uses HMAC. This is a real over-claim (RFC-3161 = third-party non-repudiation vs. HMAC = on-box tamper-evidence the secret-holder can backdate).

I dropped to LOW because the same documents already carry correct honest-scope notes that contradict the over-claim (GOVERNANCE.md:73-76, the preregistration.py module docstring, and ADR-0009), so this is a localized internal inconsistency in two body sentences rather than systemic deception, and it has zero impact on engine behavior or security.
